content.text:
Compactness is one of the most important concepts in analysis. It captures 
a notion of "finiteness" that applies even to infinite sets.

content.title: Open Cover
content.content:
An **open cover** of a set $S$ is a collection 
$\{O_\alpha\}$ of open sets whose union contains $S$:
\[
S \subseteq \bigcup_\alpha O_\alpha.
\]
A **finite subcover** is a finite subcollection that still covers $S$.

content.title: Compact Set
content.content:
A set $K \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ is **compact** if 
every open cover of $K$ has a finite subcover.

content.title: Compact and Non-Compact Sets
content.problem:
1. $[0, 1]$ is compact (we will prove this).

2. $(0, 1)$ is *not* compact: $\{(1/n, 1) : n \geq 2\}$ covers $(0,1)$ 
but has no finite subcover.

3. $\mathbb{R}$ is not compact: $\{(-n, n) : n \in \mathbb{N}\}$ has no 
finite subcover.

content.title: Heine-Borel Theorem
content.content: A subset of $\mathbb{R}$ is compact if and only if it is closed and bounded.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
$(\Rightarrow)$ **Compact $\Rightarrow$ Bounded:** The cover 
$\{(-n, n) : n \in \mathbb{N}\}$ has a finite subcover $\{(-N, N)\}$, so 
$K \subseteq (-N, N)$.

**Compact $\Rightarrow$ Closed:** Let $x \notin K$. For each $y \in K$, 
let $\varepsilon_y = |x - y|/2$ and let $O_y = V_{\varepsilon_y}(y)$. The 
collection $\{O_y : y \in K\}$ covers $K$; take a finite subcover 
$\{O_{y_1}, \ldots, O_{y_n}\}$. Then 
$\varepsilon = \min\{\varepsilon_{y_1}, \ldots, \varepsilon_{y_n}\}$ gives 
$V_\varepsilon(x) \cap K = \emptyset$. So $x$ is not a limit point of $K$.

$(\Leftarrow)$ Let $K$ be closed and bounded, say $K \subseteq [a, b]$. 
We prove $[a, b]$ is compact; then $K$, as a closed subset of a compact set, 
is compact.

Suppose $\{O_\alpha\}$ covers $[a, b]$ but has no finite subcover. Bisect 
$[a, b]$ into $[a, (a+b)/2]$ and $[(a+b)/2, b]$. At least one half has no 
finite subcover; call it $I_1$. Continue bisecting to get nested intervals 
$I_n$ with $|I_n| = (b-a)/2^n$ and no $I_n$ having a finite subcover.

By the Nested Interval Property, $\bigcap I_n = \{x\}$ for some $x \in [a,b]$. 
Then $x \in O_\alpha$ for some $\alpha$. Since $O_\alpha$ is open, some 
$V_\varepsilon(x) \subseteq O_\alpha$. For large $n$, $I_n \subseteq V_\varepsilon(x) \subseteq O_\alpha$, 
so $I_n$ is covered by the single set $O_\alpha$—contradiction.

content.title: Sequential Compactness
content.content:
A set $K \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ is compact if and only if every sequence 
in $K$ has a subsequence converging to a point in $K$.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
$(\Rightarrow)$ By Heine-Borel, $K$ is closed and bounded. By 
Bolzano-Weierstrass, any sequence in $K$ has a convergent subsequence. 
Since $K$ is closed, the limit is in $K$.

$(\Leftarrow)$ If $K$ is not bounded, there exists $(x_n)$ in $K$ with 
$|x_n| \to \infty$; this has no convergent subsequence. If $K$ is not 
closed, there exists $x_n \to x$ with $x_n \in K$ but $x \notin K$; every 
subsequence converges to $x \notin K$.

content.title: Properties of Compact Sets
content.content:
1. Every closed subset of a compact set is compact.

2. The intersection of a collection of compact sets is compact.

3. The union of finitely many compact sets is compact.

4. If $K$ is compact and $F$ is closed, then $K \cap F$ is compact.
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Eduard Heine (1821--1881) and Émile Borel (1871--1956)
content.content:
The Heine-Borel theorem, though bearing both names, was proved in various 
forms by several mathematicians. Borel's 1895 paper established the result 
for countable covers; Heine's earlier work on uniform continuity implicitly 
used similar ideas.
